region_of_origin: Southeast Asia and South Asia (widely cultivated across tropical and subtropical regions)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of raw taro is approximately 150g, equivalent to one medium-sized taro root, before peeling and cooking.

nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for raw taro (Colocasia esculenta). Confidence 0.95 as reported in input record. gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Taro (Colocasia esculenta)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 54 (low GI) for taro is consistent with published values in the University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature (Atkinson et al., 2021, Am J Clin Nutr). Glycemic load per 150g serving calculated as (54 × 26.46 × 1.5) / 100 = 21.5 for 150g; input record states 14.3 which corresponds to a 100g reference portion: (54 × 26.46) / 100 = 14.3. GL per serving retained as 14.3 per 100g reference portion consistent with input.

hypertension.key_factor: Very low sodium (11mg/100g) and exceptionally high potassium (591mg/100g) yielding a highly favourable Na:K ratio consistent with DASH dietary principles
hypertension.guidance: Raw taro is an excellent choice for hypertensive individuals due to its very low sodium content (11mg/100g) and high potassium (591mg/100g); prepare by boiling or steaming without added salt, and avoid high-sodium sauces or large quantities of coconut milk to preserve its favourable blood-pressure profile.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate content (26.46g/100g) offset by high dietary fibre (4.1g/100g), very low sugar (0.4g/100g), and a low glycemic index of 54
type2_diabetes.guidance: Taro has a low GI of 54 and high fibre content that moderates postprandial glucose response; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portions to 100–150g cooked per meal, pair with non-starchy vegetables and lean protein, and avoid deep-frying or sweetened preparations to manage total carbohydrate load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Negligible saturated fat (0.04g/100g), zero cholesterol, high dietary fibre (4.1g/100g), and high potassium (591mg/100g) supporting cardiovascular health
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Raw taro is cardioprotective due to its negligible saturated fat, zero cholesterol, high potassium, and soluble fibre content; it is suitable for cardiovascular disease management when prepared without added fats or high-sodium condiments, and its fibre may contribute to modest LDL-cholesterol reduction.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Virtually fat-free, no trans fat, dietary fibre supporting gut microbiome and immune regulation, plus potassium and calcium relevant to bone health
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Taro is suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis when thoroughly cooked, as raw taro contains calcium oxalate crystals that cause mucosal irritation; cooking neutralises oxalate irritants and preserves its anti-inflammatory fibre and bone-relevant mineral content.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Taro (Colocasia esculenta), known as raw taro or taro root, is a starchy tuberous root vegetable widely cultivated and consumed across Southeast Asia, South Asia, East Asia, the Pacific Islands, and the Caribbean. It has a brown, rough outer skin and white to pale purple flesh with a firm, dense texture when raw. Raw taro has a mild, slightly nutty, starchy flavour that becomes sweeter and creamier upon cooking. It must always be cooked before consumption, as raw taro contains calcium oxalate crystals that cause significant oral and gastrointestinal irritation. Taro is a significant source of complex carbohydrates (26.46g/100g) and dietary fibre (4.1g/100g), with a low glycemic index of approximately 54, making it a more glycaemically favourable starchy staple compared to white rice or potato. It is exceptionally rich in potassium (591mg/100g), which supports blood pressure regulation via the DASH dietary pattern, and provides moderate calcium (43mg/100g) and iron (0.55mg/100g). Its very low sodium content (11mg/100g), negligible saturated fat (0.04g/100g), and zero cholesterol make it suitable for hypertension and cardiovascular disease management. In Singapore and across Asia, taro is used in savoury dishes such as yam rice, braised pork with taro, and taro cake (wu tou gao), as well as in desserts such as taro paste, taro ice cream, and orh nee (Teochew taro dessert). It is a staple ingredient in Cantonese, Teochew, Hakka, and Southeast Asian cuisines. Nutritionally, taro is vegetarian, vegan, gluten-free, halal, and kosher, and is classified as a high-fibre, low-sodium, plant-based starchy vegetable.

What is the glycemic index of taro?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database with confidence 0.95 as reported in the input record. GI value of 54 is consistent with University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature. Saturated fat and detailed fatty acid profile cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (FDC ID 170071) for taro raw. All values are internally consistent and plausible for raw taro.
notes: 1. Primary nutrient data sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo database (confidence 0.95). Saturated fat (0.04g), monounsaturated fat (0.01g), polyunsaturated fat (0.08g), trans fat (0.0g), and micronutrient values (vitamin C, vitamin A, B-vitamins, vitamin D, vitamin E, phosphorus, magnesium, zinc, selenium, water) cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for Taro, raw (FDC ID 170071) where HPB did not report these fields. 2. Trans fat is 0.0g: confirmed zero — taro is a raw plant food containing no trans fatty acids. Cholesterol is 0.0g: confirmed zero — taro is a plant food and contains no dietary cholesterol. Vitamin B12 is 0.0g: naturally zero — taro is a plant food and does not contain vitamin B12. Vitamin D is 0.0g: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy for raw taro. 3. Glycemic load of 14.3 is calculated on a 100g reference portion: (54 × 26.46) / 100 = 14.3. 4. Raw taro must always be cooked before consumption due to calcium oxalate crystals causing oral and gastrointestinal irritation. 5. Taro is a globally cultivated crop; region of origin is attributed to Southeast Asia and South Asia with wide cultivation across tropical and subtropical regions. 6. Multilingual names provided for Chinese (simplified and traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi reflecting the primary consumer communities in Singapore and the broader Asian region. 7. FoodEx2 code A00LC and FoodOn URI FOODON_03301710 are used as defensible ontology anchors for raw taro; SNOMED CT code 256329004 is used for the taro substance concept.
